checks.a_analysis: The text content in the sections provided (Cover, Page 2, Page 3, Appendix A, and Appendix C) aligns with the OCR provided for those pages. No hallucinated body text or invented headers within the sections were found. The metadata title and image alt text are generated as per pipeline standards and are acceptable.

I verified the phone numbers and addresses in Appendix A (Pages 31-34) against the source PDF. Every number (e.g., Stan Duncan 828/697-4576, Chris Estes 919/754-6575) and address matches the source. No URLs or email addresses were found in the output outside of the source material.

The output is missing a significant portion of the document. Specifically, pages 4-30 and page 35 are not present in the 'sections' array. This includes the Introduction, Section I (Purpose), Section II (Accomplishments), Section III (Action Plan), and Appendix B (Establishing Authority). While the JSON includes a key '_omitted' as a placeholder, the authoritative content from these pages is completely absent from the converted output.
checks.b_evidence_source: Page 4 (NC OneMap intro), Page 5 (Contents), Page 6 (Introduction), Page 7 (Section I), Page 10 (Section II), Page 26 (Section III), Page 35 (Appendix B)
checks.b_evidence_output: The 'sections' array jumps from Page 3 (reading_order 26) to Appendix A (reading_order 274), and from Page 34 (reading_order 281) to Appendix C (reading_order 291).

checks.a_analysis: I compared the tables in Appendix A and Appendix C. In Appendix A, all rows from pages 31-34 are present. In Appendix C, I counted 27 data rows on page 36, 31 rows on page 37, and 10 rows on page 38 in both the source and the output. Although page 37's first data row was treated as a header in the output, the information content for all rows is preserved.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: For the sections included (Cover, Page 3, Appendices), the text is complete. The Executive Summary on Page 3 contains all paragraphs and list items from the source. The tables in the appendices have full row content.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: Wording and numerical data in the provided sections match the source. Minor reformatting such as combining columns in the member tables (Page 31) or treating a data row as a header row in a continued table (Page 37) are acceptable and do not alter meaning.

checks.a_analysis: The content in the 'Executive Summary' (page 3) and 'Accomplishments' (page 10) is repeated in the source document. The output correctly reflects this without creating additional, unintentional duplications within the structure.

The heading hierarchy is preserved. The document title is rendered as H1 on page 1, major section titles like 'Executive Summary' and 'Appendix A' are rendered as H2, and sub-categories within sections are H3. While multiple H1s are used on the title page for different lines of the title, this is acceptable for accessibility and correctly reflects the cover structure.

There are two structural defects in the tables. First, for tables spanning multiple pages (Appendix A and Appendix C), the pipeline incorrectly designated the first data row on each new page as a header row (`is_header_row: true`) instead of data. Second, in Section 34, the 'Advisory Members' row contains only one cell while the table structure defines three columns, leading to a structural column count mismatch.
checks.b_evidence_source: Source Page 32: 'Edward S. Finley, Jr.' (Data row). Source Page 34: 'Advisory Members' (Internal table section header).
checks.b_evidence_output: Section 32, element reading_order 277, row index 0 has cell_type 'data' but is_header_row is 'true'. Section 34, element reading_order 281, row index 2 contains only 1 cell for a 3-column table.

checks.a_analysis: The reading order faithfully follows the source PDF's organization, moving through the title page, executive summary, main body (represented by omitted sections), and into the appendices in the correct order.
The list structure in the Executive Summary (page 3) is correctly converted to unordered lists. The nesting and item content match the source accurately.

The visible sections do not contain links. While URLs exist in the text of the source document, the output correctly captures them as part of the paragraph text without fabricating or breaking external links.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output faithfully reproduces the visual organization of the source PDF. The document structure, including the cover page information, executive summary, sections I through III, and the appendices, follows the original flow. The transition from the PDF's fixed-page layout to a responsive HTML format is handled well, with headers, text blocks, and figures appearing in their correct relative positions. Lists and sidebars (like the 'NC OneMap' sidebar on PDF page 6) are integrated into the main flow appropriately.

The tables in the document are rendered correctly and remain highly readable. Specifically, the member lists in Appendix A (PDF pages 31-34), which are presented as column-aligned text in the PDF, are converted into well-structured HTML tables with clear headers and alternating row shading for improved readability. The data update table in Appendix C (PDF pages 36-38) is also correctly rendered as a table with aligned columns for 'TITLE' and 'CUSTODIAN'. No overlapping content or broken alignments were observed.

All meaningful images from the source PDF are present in the HTML output. This includes the organizational logos (NCGICC), the complex diagrams (Figure 1, Figure 2), all geographic maps (Figure 4, Figure 7), screenshots of the portal (Figure 6), and all example orthoimagery photos (Figures 3 and 5, and the unnumbered photos of Lincolnton, Spruce Pine, etc.). Captions for figures are correctly associated with their respective images.

A careful visual review of the rendered HTML content confirms that there is no duplicated body content. Each paragraph, table, and figure appears exactly once in the document body. While headings appear in the Table of Contents and again in the body, this is an intentional accessibility feature and does not constitute content duplication.
